title: Check Constant Ratio in Table
description: Determine whether a table represents a proportional relationship by verifying that the ratio of corresponding values is constant.
condition: A table of paired values (x, y) is given and the task is to determine if the relationship is proportional.
response: Compute y/x (or equivalent unit rate) for each row and verify whether all ratios are equal; conclude proportional if constant, otherwise not proportional.
label: Principle / Model
example: For (40, 740) and (20, 370): \(740/40 = 18.5\) and \(370/20 = 18.5\). Since the ratios are equal, the relationship is proportional.
notes: Core test for proportionality in tables.

title: Compare Rates by Steepness
description: Determine which of two proportional relationships has a greater rate by comparing the steepness (slope) of their lines on a graph.
condition: Two or more lines on a graph of proportional relationships (through the origin) representing quantities over time or similar context.
response: Identify the line that is steeper as having the greater constant of proportionality (faster rate).
label: Principle / Model
example: If line B rises more quickly than line A from $(0,0)$, then $\text{rate}_B > \text{rate}_A$.

title: Compute Missing Value in Proportional Table
description: Use the constant of proportionality to calculate an unknown value in a proportional relationship.
condition: Given a proportional relationship with known constant of proportionality k and a value for one quantity (e.g., x), and the corresponding value for the other quantity is missing.
response: Multiply or divide by k to find the missing value (e.g., y = kx or x = y/k).
label: Principle / Model
example: If the constant of proportionality is $k = 8$ and there are $16$ cups of white paint, then cups of black paint $= 16 \div 8 = 2$.

title: Compute Unit Rate from a Point
description: Find the constant of proportionality by dividing output by input from a point on a proportional graph or table.
condition: Given a point $(x,y)$ on a proportional relationship (line through origin) with $x \ne 0$.
label: Principle / Model

title: Determine if Graph Represents Proportional Relationship
description: Decide whether a graphed relationship is proportional by checking key structural features.
condition: Given a graph of a relationship between two variables.
response: State whether the relationship is proportional based on whether the graph is a straight line through the origin.
label: Principle / Model
example: A straight line passing through $(0,0)$ represents $y = kx$, so it is proportional; a line not through the origin is not proportional.
notes: Combines linearity and origin check as defining features of proportional relationships.

title: Evaluate Equation by Substitution
description: Determine the value of one variable by substituting a given value into an equation.
condition: An equation relating two variables (e.g., \(y = 16 \div x\)) and a specific value of x are given.
response: Substitute the given x-value into the equation and compute the corresponding y-value.
label: Principle / Model

title: Generate Equivalent Ratio
description: Create a new pair of values that maintains the same ratio by multiplying or dividing both quantities by the same nonzero factor.
condition: Given a ratio or proportional pair (x, y).
response: Multiply or divide both x and y by the same nonzero number to produce an equivalent ratio.
label: Principle / Model
example: From $(1, 8)$, multiply both by $10$ to get $(10, 80)$.

title: Identify Constant of Proportionality from Graph
description: Find the constant of proportionality (unit rate) from a graph of a proportional relationship.
condition: Given a straight-line graph through the origin representing a proportional relationship.
response: Compute the constant of proportionality as $k = \frac{y}{x}$ using any point on the graph (often the point where $x=1$).
label: Principle / Model
notes: Equivalent to finding slope when the line passes through the origin.

title: Interpret Constant of Proportionality in Context
description: Explain the meaning of the constant of proportionality in terms of the quantities and units in a real-world situation.
condition: Given a constant of proportionality k relating two contextual quantities.
response: State the multiplicative comparison in words, including units (e.g., 'for every 1 unit of x, there are k units of y').
label: Principle / Model
example: If $k = 8$ cups of white per cup of black, then for every 1 cup of black paint, mix 8 cups of white paint.

title: Interpret Point on Graph in Context
description: Translate a coordinate point on a graph into a contextual statement about the situation.
condition: Given a labeled point $(x, y)$ on a graph with labeled axes and units.
response: State what the ordered pair means in context, matching $x$ to the horizontal quantity and $y$ to the vertical quantity.
label: Rule / Plan
example: The point $(4, 1\tfrac{1}{3})$ means that in 4 minutes, the cyclist travels $1\tfrac{1}{3}$ miles.
notes: Requires mapping coordinates to axis labels and units.

title: Invert a Proportional Relationship
description: Given an equation of the form a = k m, rewrite it to solve for m by dividing by the constant k.
condition: An equation of the form $a = k m$ and a known value of a.
response: Divide both sides by k to isolate m (i.e., $m = \frac{a}{k}$).
label: Principle / Model
notes: Requires understanding inverse operations in proportional equations.

title: Label Axes with Quantities and Units
description: Assign appropriate variable names and units to the horizontal and vertical axes based on context.
condition: Graph of a contextual proportional relationship with unlabeled axes.
response: Label axes with correct quantities and units consistent with the situation.
label: Rule / Plan
example: Horizontal axis: bananas (cups); Vertical axis: strawberries (cups).
notes: Rationale marked No because labeling is conventional rather than derivable.???

title: Recognize Graph Passing Through Origin
description: Identify whether a graph passes through the origin $(0,0)$.
condition: Given a graph on coordinate axes.
response: Determine whether the graph includes the point $(0,0)$.
label: Category
example: Graph B passes through $(0,0)$; Graph A does not.
notes: Key visual feature required for proportional relationships.

title: Recognize Linear Graph
description: Identify whether a graph is a straight line, indicating a constant rate of change.
condition: Given a graph of a relationship.
response: Determine whether the graph forms a straight line.
label: Category
example: Graph C is not linear because it bends; Graph B is linear because it is a single straight line.

title: Recognize Non-Proportional Relationship from Variable Ratio
description: Identify that a relationship is not proportional when the ratio y/x is not constant.
condition: Ratios y/x are computed for multiple pairs and are not equal.
response: Conclude that the relationship is not proportional.
label: Concept
example: For \(x=1,2,4,8\) in \(y=16/x\): \(y/x = 16, 4, 1, 0.25\). Since the ratios differ, the relationship is not proportional.

title: Solve One-Step Multiplicative Equation
description: Solve an equation where a variable is multiplied by a constant by using multiplication or division.
condition: Equation of the form $y = kx$ or $a = km$ with one known value substituted.
response: Multiply or divide to compute the unknown variable value.
label: Rule / Plan
notes: Includes both forward substitution and solving via division.

title: Write Equation from Constant of Proportionality
description: Express a proportional relationship as an equation using the constant of proportionality.
condition: Given constant of proportionality $k$ relating variables $x$ and $y$ proportionally.
response: Write equation in form $y = kx$ with variables defined.
label: Principle / Model
